methodology.description: Each SaaS company is scored on three disruption factors (1-10 scale). The composite Disruption Score is a weighted average.

domainExpertiseOverlap.description: How much domain expertise do end users have in the area the software serves? Higher = users can more easily build AI alternatives. 10 = users are developers/technical experts who could vibe-code replacements. 1 = users have no technical ability to create alternatives.
domainExpertiseOverlap.examples: Atlassian (9): devs use it and can build alternatives. Veeva (2): pharma compliance users lack software skills.

smallBusinessExposure.description: What fraction of revenue comes from small businesses vs enterprises? Higher = more SMB exposure = higher disruption risk (SMBs more likely to switch to AI-native tools). 10 = almost entirely SMB. 1 = almost entirely large enterprise.
smallBusinessExposure.examples: Squarespace (9): mostly small businesses. Workday (2): large enterprises.

standardSetterDefensibility.description: Is the company a de facto standard in its domain? Higher = more defensible = LOWER disruption risk. Score is inverted for the composite: 10 = strong standard setter (low risk), 1 = no standard-setting power (high risk).
standardSetterDefensibility.examples: Adobe (9): Creative Suite is the standard. Freshworks (2): competes in crowded space with no standard-setting power.

methodology.interpretation: Higher composite score = more vulnerable to AI disruption. Range: 1-10.

companies.name: Atlassian
companies.reasoning: End users are developers who can build alternatives. Mix of SMB and enterprise. Jira is a near-standard but alternatives exist.

companies.name: Salesforce
companies.reasoning: Sales reps aren't builders. Heavily enterprise. CRM category leader and platform standard.

companies.name: Adobe
companies.reasoning: Designers are creative but not coders. Mixed customer base. Photoshop/Illustrator are the standard.

companies.name: Shopify
companies.reasoning: Merchants are business-savvy but not technical. Heavily SMB. Strong brand but Wix/Squarespace compete.

companies.name: Workday
companies.reasoning: HR users aren't technical. Almost entirely large enterprise. Strong position in enterprise HCM.

companies.name: ServiceNow
companies.reasoning: IT admins are technical. Very enterprise-heavy. ITSM standard in large orgs.

companies.name: Datadog
companies.reasoning: DevOps engineers are highly technical. Mix but enterprise-leaning. Strong but Grafana/New Relic compete.

companies.name: Snowflake
companies.reasoning: Data engineers are very technical. Enterprise-heavy. Strong but Databricks/BigQuery compete.

companies.name: HubSpot
companies.reasoning: Marketers are savvy but not builders. SMB-focused. Strong in SMB CRM but not a standard.

companies.name: Twilio
companies.reasoning: Users are developers building on the API. Mixed customer base. Leading API but competitors exist.

companies.name: Zoom
companies.reasoning: End users are non-technical. Broad customer base. Well-known but Teams/Meet compete hard.

companies.name: Cloudflare
companies.reasoning: DevOps/infra engineers are highly technical. Mix of SMB and enterprise. Strong edge platform.

companies.name: MongoDB
companies.reasoning: Users are developers. Mixed customer base. Popular but Postgres/DynamoDB compete.

companies.name: Figma
companies.reasoning: Designers creative but not coders. Mixed base. Becoming the design collaboration standard.

companies.name: Notion
companies.reasoning: Tech-savvy users but not all builders. SMB-leaning. Popular but Google Docs/Confluence compete.

companies.name: Canva
companies.reasoning: Non-designers using templates. Very SMB. Strong brand but AI image tools are direct competitors.

companies.name: Squarespace
companies.reasoning: Non-technical users building websites. Almost all SMB. No standard-setting power.

companies.name: GitLab
companies.reasoning: Pure developer tool. Mix but enterprise-leaning. Competes with GitHub.

companies.name: Veeva Systems
companies.reasoning: Pharma/biotech compliance users aren't technical. All enterprise. Dominant in life sciences vertical.

companies.name: Palantir
companies.reasoning: Analysts are semi-technical. Government/enterprise only. Deep moat in defense/intel.

companies.name: Freshworks
companies.reasoning: Support agents aren't builders. SMB-heavy. No standard-setting power, crowded market.

companies.name: Asana
companies.reasoning: Mixed user base, some technical. Mid-market focus. Competes with Monday, Jira, Notion.

companies.name: DocuSign
companies.reasoning: Users just sign documents. Mixed base. De facto e-signature standard.

companies.name: Okta
companies.reasoning: IT/security admins are technical. Enterprise-heavy. Strong in identity space.

companies.name: Monday.com
companies.reasoning: Non-technical project managers. SMB-leaning. No standard-setting power.

companies.name: Elastic
companies.reasoning: Developers and DevOps engineers. Enterprise-leaning. Elasticsearch is well-known but open-source competitors exist.

companies.name: Wix
companies.reasoning: Non-technical small business owners. Almost all SMB. No standard-setting power.

companies.name: Zendesk
companies.reasoning: Support agents aren't technical. Mixed base. Known brand but AI chatbots are direct competitors.

companies.name: Confluent
companies.reasoning: Pure developer/data engineer tool. Enterprise-heavy. Kafka is the standard but self-hosted is an option.

companies.name: UiPath
companies.reasoning: Business analysts with some technical skill. Enterprise-heavy. RPA leader but AI agents are direct replacements.

companies.name: Intuit
companies.reasoning: Small business owners and individuals. Very SMB. QuickBooks/TurboTax are standards.

companies.name: Autodesk
companies.reasoning: Engineers are technical but in physical domains. Mixed base. AutoCAD/Revit are industry standards.
